name: lgrepDaemonOperationalSafety
title: LgrepDaemonOperationalSafety
purpose: Capability: LgrepDaemonOperationalSafety — defines runtime invariants and operator-facing diagnostics that keep the shared lgrep MCP daemon bounded, observable, and safe under concurrent OpenCode/Vision sessions.

requirements.title: Expensive daemon work is tracked as structured runtime jobs
requirements.body: Every expensive blocking operation submitted by the daemon MUST be represented by a structured runtime job before it enters the blocking executor. Job records MUST include a stable id, kind, caller/tool, project path when applicable, status, created/started/finished timestamps when available, and bounded error summary. Diagnostics MUST read these records directly instead of inferring correctness from process names, thread names, CPU percentage, logs, or prose.

scenarios.title: Job is visible while running
given: An indexing, search, status, watcher, or maintenance operation that submits blocking work
then: A runtime job record exists before the blocking call runs
The record includes id, kind, caller, project path when applicable, status, and timestamps
The diagnostics surface reports the job from runtime state

scenarios.title: Diagnostics do not infer job state heuristically
given: A daemon process with active runtime jobs
then: Job status comes from structured runtime records
CPU percentage, process names, thread names, and logs are not the authority for job lifecycle correctness

requirements.title: Timeout and cancellation leave terminal observable job state
requirements.body: When a caller times out or cancels while blocking daemon work may still be running, lgrep MUST NOT claim the underlying synchronous work was killed unless it was actually cancelled before starting. Running work that cannot be safely killed MUST be marked abandoned or cancellation-requested, then transitioned to a terminal observed state when the underlying future later finishes or fails. No job may remain active forever after timeout, cancellation, or shutdown cleanup.

scenarios.title: Timed-out running job is marked abandoned
given: A blocking runtime job is running in the daemon executor
scenarios.when: The awaiting MCP tool coroutine times out before the blocking function returns
then: The tool returns a structured timeout error
The job status becomes abandoned or cancellation-requested
Diagnostics show the job is no longer an ordinary active job

scenarios.title: Abandoned job records eventual completion
given: A blocking job was marked abandoned after caller timeout
scenarios.when: The underlying blocking function later returns or raises
then: The job transitions to a terminal finished-after-abandon or failed-after-abandon state
The terminal record remains available in bounded recent-job diagnostics

scenarios.title: Queued job cancellation is represented honestly
given: A job is queued but not yet running
scenarios.when: Shutdown or timeout cancels the queued future before it starts
then: The job reaches a terminal cancelled state
The response and diagnostics do not imply a running thread was killed

requirements.title: Global semantic status is cheap and bounded by default
requirements.body: Calling status_semantic with no project path MUST return a cheap bounded summary from in-memory daemon state. It MUST NOT fan out into deep per-project LanceDB counts, disk scans, index refreshes, or other blocking storage work for every loaded project by default. Exact deep file/chunk counts MUST require a scoped project path or another explicit bounded operation.

scenarios.title: No-arg status avoids deep per-project storage calls
given: A daemon with multiple loaded projects
scenarios.when: status_semantic is called with an empty path
then: The response is produced from in-memory project/runtime state
The handler does not call LanceDB count/indexed-file methods for every project
The response indicates that deep stats are omitted or memory-sourced

scenarios.title: Scoped status remains exact and bounded
given: A daemon with a loaded project
scenarios.when: status_semantic is called with that project's path
then: The response may perform exact per-project file/chunk reads
The work is bounded to the requested project
The response preserves the single-project status contract

requirements.title: Daemon diagnostics expose operator-safe runtime health
requirements.body: The daemon MUST expose a cheap read-only diagnostics surface that reports process/runtime identity, loaded projects, active/recent runtime jobs, timeout/abandonment state, and bounded executor configuration. Diagnostics MAY include full local project paths for local support workflows, but MUST NOT include environment variables, API keys, request payload secrets, or raw tracebacks.

scenarios.title: Diagnostics report runtime health
then: The response includes PID or process identity, uptime, transport, worker limit, loaded project count, and job counts
Active jobs include kind, project path, caller, status, and age
Recent terminal jobs include final status and bounded error summary when applicable

scenarios.title: Diagnostics exclude secrets
given: A daemon with VOYAGE_API_KEY or other environment values configured
then: The response contains no API key values
The response contains no environment map
The response contains no raw request payload secrets
The response contains no raw traceback text

requirements.title: Blocking daemon work uses bounded executor ownership
requirements.body: Blocking work submitted by daemon tools and daemon-owned watchers MUST use an lgrep-owned bounded executor or runtime supervisor rather than uncontrolled default-executor fanout. The worker limit MUST be configurable with a safe default. When capacity is exhausted, the daemon MUST queue or reject work with structured state/error information instead of spawning unbounded workers invisibly.

scenarios.title: Worker limit is bounded and visible
given: A daemon starts with default configuration
then: The configured worker limit is reported
Blocking jobs submitted by semantic/status/watcher/maintenance paths use the owned runtime supervisor

scenarios.title: Capacity pressure is explicit
given: The runtime supervisor is at or beyond its configured blocking-work capacity
then: The job is queued or rejected according to a documented policy
Diagnostics expose queued/rejected state or error classification
The daemon does not create unbounded default-executor workers

requirements.title: Shared HTTP and Vision deployment guidance is explicit
requirements.body: Documentation and installer guidance for shared streamable-http or Vision-managed deployments MUST describe safe local defaults and troubleshooting for high CPU/thread incidents. Guidance MUST cover explicit warm paths, auto-warm behavior, worktree deduplication, tool timeouts, worker limits, diagnostics, and the distinction between local stdio and shared daemon mode.

scenarios.title: Shared deployment settings are documented
given: A user reads lgrep setup documentation for Vision or shared HTTP
scenarios.when: They look for safe multi-session settings
then: The docs mention LGREP_WARM_PATHS, LGREP_AUTO_WARM_DISK, LGREP_WORKTREE_DEDUP, LGREP_TOOL_TIMEOUT_S, and the worker-limit setting
The docs explain when to use stdio versus shared HTTP

scenarios.title: High CPU/thread troubleshooting uses diagnostics
given: An operator sees high lgrep CPU or thread count
then: The docs direct them to the diagnostics/status surfaces
The docs explain how to interpret active/recent jobs and stale loaded projects
The docs do not require exposing secrets in support output

requirements.title: Tracked text files reject unresolved merge conflict markers
requirements.body: The repository MUST have an automated guard that fails when tracked source or documentation text files contain unresolved merge conflict markers such as <<<<<<<, =======, or >>>>>>>. The guard MUST report file and line evidence and SHOULD avoid scanning binary/cache/generated files.

scenarios.title: Conflict marker fails verification
given: A tracked source or documentation text file contains an unresolved merge conflict marker
scenarios.when: The conflict-marker guard runs in tests or CI
then: The command exits non-zero
The output includes the file path and line number

scenarios.title: Clean tracked text files pass verification
given: Tracked source and documentation text files contain no unresolved conflict markers
then: The command exits zero
